project.name: jobsync
project.description: JobSync is a self-hosted, open-source web app for tracking and organizing a job search, featuring an application tracker, monitoring dashboard, AI resume review and job matching, EURES/ESCO European job discovery automations, and a CRM module for contacts, interviews, tasks, and notes. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.languageNotes: Uses QEMU + Buildx for multi-arch builds, docker/metadata-action for semver tag derivation, gha build cache, and a concurrency group that cancels in-flight builds on newer pushes to the same ref.

nodes.name: build-and-push

tour.title: What JobSync Is
tour.description: Start with the README to learn the project's purpose: a self-hosted Next.js 15 job-application tracker with EURES/ESCO job discovery, a CRM, and a public REST API. The contributing guide explains the bun-based workflow, the i18n-everything rule, and the DDD conventions that the rest of this tour makes concrete. Read these first to get the vocabulary (Connector, Module, DiscoveredVacancy, Automation, Person) before diving into code.

tour.title: How the App Boots
tour.description: The App Router root layout is the shell every page renders inside, wiring the locale, theme, and global providers. Edge middleware gates each request (auth redirect, credential-param stripping, security headers), while `instrumentation.ts` runs once at server startup to boot the automation scheduler, the CRM cron, the event-bus consumers, and the holiday pre-warm. Together these three files are the runtime entry point of the whole system.

tour.title: Authentication & Session
tour.description: Building on the middleware gate from Step 2, NextAuth is split into an edge-safe config (`auth.config.ts`, used by middleware) and the full server config (`auth.ts`, with the Prisma adapter and providers). `getCurrentUser()` derived from this session is the trust anchor for every ownership check (ADR-015) you will see in the server actions later.
tour.languageLesson: NextAuth v5 splits config into an edge-compatible `auth.config.ts` (no Node-only deps, usable in middleware) and a full `auth.ts`. The middleware imports only the lightweight half so it can run on the Edge runtime.

tour.title: The Relational Model (Prisma Schema)
tour.description: The Prisma schema is the single source of truth for the data model — Job, Person, Automation, Notification and the CRM aggregates all live here. `db.ts` exports the singleton Prisma client (the most-depended-upon module after i18n), and the init migration plus the recruiter-triangle migration show how the schema evolved, including the Welle 3 columns that connect a Job to its recruiter Person.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma migrations are ordered, append-only SQL snapshots. Each migration applies an incremental, forward-only change; the schema file describes the desired end state while the migration files record how the database got there.

tour.title: Domain Models & Value Objects
tour.description: These models hold the domain logic that Prisma rows cannot express: `job.model.ts` and `person.model.ts` define value objects, state machines, and JSON-field parsers (emails, phones, social profiles). `actionResult.ts` defines the `ActionResult<T>` envelope every server action returns. This is the ubiquitous language the README's DDD section promises, expressed in code.

tour.title: Server Actions as Repositories (DDD)
tour.description: Server actions are the project's Repositories — one file per aggregate, each returning the `ActionResult<T>` from Step 5 and enforcing `userId` ownership on every Prisma query. `job.actions.ts` is the Job aggregate (job + notes + tags + status), and `profile.actions.ts` (one of the highest fan-out files) manages the Profile aggregate. These are the seam between the React UI and the data layer.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js exposes every export of a `"use server"` file as a browser-callable endpoint. So per ADR-019, any function taking a raw `userId` must NOT live here — it goes in a `server-only` module instead, and actions derive identity from the session, never from a client argument.

tour.title: The Connector / Module ACL
tour.description: All external integrations follow the App ↔ Connector ↔ Module anti-corruption pattern. `manifest.ts` (high fan-in) defines the `ModuleManifest` every module self-describes with, `registry.ts` is the single unified registry, and `register-all.ts` is the central import list that side-effect-registers every module at startup. This is the shared kernel that keeps EURES, OpenAI, Logo.dev and the rest pluggable.

tour.title: A Connector in Action: Job Discovery
tour.description: Following the ACL kernel from Step 7, the Job Discovery connector translates foreign APIs into the shared `DiscoveredVacancy` domain type. `types.ts` defines that contract, the EURES module is one concrete implementation behind it, and the `runner.ts` (top fan-out) orchestrates search → dedup → enrich → match with credential PUSH. New job boards plug in by declaring a manifest — no runner changes needed.

tour.title: Event Bus & Cross-Cutting Subsystems
tour.description: The TypedEventBus decouples the subsystems: server actions and the runner publish typed events, and consumers (notifications, CRM activity logging, enrichment) react. `event-bus.ts` is the dispatcher, `event-schemas.ts` provides Zod runtime validation for every payload, the `RunCoordinator` is the single mutex-guarded entry point for all automation runs, and the notification `ChannelRouter` fans dispatches out to in-app/webhook/email/push channels.
tour.languageLesson: Zod schemas in `event-schemas.ts` are bound to TypeScript interfaces via `satisfies z.ZodType<XPayload>`. Adding a field to one without the other is a compile error — so the runtime validator and the static type can never silently drift apart.

tour.title: The CRM Connection (Welle 3)
tour.description: Welle 3 wires the CRM into the Job aggregate. `JobContactPicker.tsx` is the two-tier UI that links a recruiter Person to a Job (the recruiter-triangle from the migration in Step 4), `jobContact.actions.ts` is its repository, `ActivityTimeline.tsx` renders the immutable CrmActivityLog projection built from the events in Step 9, and `blocklist-match.ts` is the pure matcher the runner uses to suppress auto-created persons by email/domain/pattern.

tour.title: The Public API v1 & GDPR-Safe AI Egress
tour.description: The `/api/v1` namespace is JobSync's Open Host Service — a deliberately designed REST surface over the data layer. `with-api-auth.ts` is the mandatory HOF wrapping every v1 route (CORS + API-key auth + IP rate limit + error sanitization), and `jobs/route.ts` is the Welle 3 jobs endpoint with explicit `select` shapes that never leak internal fields. `pii/index.ts` is the dependency-free redaction leaf that scrubs names/emails/phones before any cloud-AI transfer (GDPR Art. 5).

tour.title: Internationalization Backbone
tour.description: Every user-visible string is translated across EN/DE/FR/ES. `i18n/index.ts` is the single most-depended-upon module in the codebase (165 fan-in) — the client adapter exposing `useTranslations` and locale-aware formatters. `i18n/server.ts` is its `server-only` twin for actions and route handlers. The adapter pattern means the whole app could swap dictionary backends without touching a single consumer.

tour.title: The Presentation Layer
tour.description: With the data, domain, and i18n plumbing in place, the React UI ties it together. The dashboard layout is the authenticated shell, `JobsContainer.tsx` is the primary job-list view, and `StagingContainer.tsx` drives the Tinder-style staging deck where discovered vacancies are promoted into Jobs — every deck action routing through the `useDeckStack` state machine (ADR-030).

tour.title: Specs, Containerization & CI
tour.description: Allium specs are the authoritative source of truth for domain rules — `crm.allium` and `notification-dispatch.allium` capture behavior that the code in Steps 9-10 implements. The Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build to ship a lean production image, docker-compose orchestrates the self-hosted stack, and the CI pipeline runs the test suite and type-check on every push.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use several `FROM` stages: a `deps`/`builder` stage installs dev dependencies and compiles, while the final `runner` stage copies only the built artifacts into a minimal image — cutting the production image size dramatically.

tour.title: How It's Tested
tour.description: JobSync ships nothing without tests. `blocklist-match.spec.ts` pins the pure CRM matcher from Step 10, `JobContactPicker.spec.tsx` mounts the Welle 3 contact-picker UI, and `parse-salary-range.spec.ts` exercises a pure domain library. These illustrate the test pyramid — pure-function unit tests, component tests, and the regression guards that protect the architecture you've just toured.
